---
title: "Is my USB Stick fried?"
date: 2011-02-15
forum: Hardware
---

### Post by kaefert66014235 on 2011-02-15
I've got a SanDisk Titanium CRUZER 512MB USB-Stick.
Until a view hours ago it worked perfectly (for years), than I stuck it into a big printer machine to scan to it, which seemed to work fine. After returning with the USB-Stick to my Laptop it didn't seem to work anymore. I then tried it with no luck on several different Windows and Ubuntu computers in several different USB Ports.
I've got a SanDisk Titanium CRUZER 512MB USB-Stick.
Until a view hours ago it worked perfectly (for years), than I stuck it into a big printer machine to scan to it, which seemed to work fine. After returning with the USB-Stick to my Laptop it didn't seem to work anymore. I then tried it with no luck on several different Windows and Ubuntu computers in several different USB Ports.

I think its fried.. I would like to investigate. here are my findings, I would need help interpreting them.

dmesg output:
```
[  522.000308] usb 2-1: new high speed USB device using ehci_hcd and address 3
[  522.070607] hub 2-0:1.0: unable to enumerate USB device on port 1
[  522.390093] usb 5-1: new full speed USB device using uhci_hcd and address 2
[  522.520236] usb 5-1: device descriptor read/64, error -71
[  522.760256] usb 5-1: device descriptor read/64, error -71
[  522.990355] usb 5-1: new full speed USB device using uhci_hcd and address 3
[  523.120331] usb 5-1: device descriptor read/64, error -71
[  523.360263] usb 5-1: device descriptor read/64, error -71
[  523.590338] usb 5-1: new full speed USB device using uhci_hcd and address 4
[  524.010233] usb 5-1: device not accepting address 4, error -71
[  524.130261] usb 5-1: new full speed USB device using uhci_hcd and address 5
[  524.560086] usb 5-1: device not accepting address 5, error -71
[  524.560166] hub 5-0:1.0: unable to enumerate USB device on port 1
[  638.584988] lo: Disabled Privacy Extensions
```

on sticking the USB-Stick into a USB-Port /sys/kernel/debug/usb/usbmon/0u gives:
```
ffff88007a85e240 2702534624 C Ii:2:001:1 0:2048 1 = 02
ffff88007a85e240 2702534667 S Ii:2:001:1 -115:2048 4 <
```

on pulling the USB-Stick out of the USB-Port /sys/kernel/debug/usb/usbmon/0u gives:
```
ffff880051b850c0 3085655338 S Ci:5:001:0 s a3 00 0000 0001 0004 4 <
ffff880051b850c0 3085655379 C Ci:5:001:0 0 4 = 00010100
ffff880051b850c0 3085655386 S Co:5:001:0 s 23 01 0010 0001 0000 0

ffff88007abb6300 3088525411 C Ii:5:001:1 -2:128 0
```

The only change from lsusb I could produce with my USB-Stick was from
   Port 1: 0000.0100 power
to -->
   Port 1: 0000.0101 power connect
within the entry of an Linux Foundation 1.1 root hub

Can somebody help me? It would be great to get the data from the stick back... And I'm kind of afraid of sticking another good USB-Stick into that USB-Stick killing printing machine to scan my notes again...

---

### Post by tgalati4 on 2011-02-15
It could be the controller on the flash drive went bad--perhaps static electricity zapped it.  Could be overvoltage from the printer USB port--you might want to verify with a voltmeter--the two outside pins should be 5.0VDC.  If you really need the data, and it won't mount, find another, similar flash drive and perform some surgery.  It's not pretty, but I have tried it once just to see if it was possible.

If nothing else, try resoldering the contacts.  The flexing of the tip from pulling it in and out can weaken the solder joints.  Just a simple touch up on 4 spots may be all that is needed.

If the thumb drive is old, been in pockets, and the printer usb port is tight, then that would point to solder joint issues.  Time to crack it open.

---

### Post by kaefert66014235 on 2011-02-17
well its not my private printer but a shared one of the school I work at. It would look a bit strange if I go and check the voltage of its USB-Port..

The data was not important enough that I would invest that much time to find another one of the same type and switch the flash chips.. that would be a lot of connectors!

The 4 USB Pins are probably connected to the board, so its probably the controller..
